questions.question: What is the main purpose of an enum?
options: To replace magic numbers with named, self-documenting constants
questions.correctAnswer: To replace magic numbers with named, self-documenting constants
questions.explanation: Note: 'An enum is a set of named integer constants. Instead of passing magic numbers, you pass names with meaning.'

questions.question: Why can't two plain enums share member names in the same scope?
options: Values leak into the surrounding scope — name collision
questions.correctAnswer: Values leak into the surrounding scope — name collision
questions.explanation: Note: 'enum Color { Red }; enum TrafficLight { Red }; // ERROR — Red already defined!'

questions.question: What are two key advantages of `enum class` over plain `enum`?
questions.correctAnswer: Strongly typed (no implicit conversion to int) and scoped (must use prefix)
acceptableAnswers: no implicit int conversion and scoped
questions.explanation: Note: 'enum class is strongly typed and scoped. Values don't leak, don't convert to int implicitly.'

questions.question: How do you convert an `enum class` value to its underlying integer?
questions.correctAnswer: static_cast<int>(value) or C++23's std::to_underlying(value)
questions.explanation: Note: 'int x = static_cast<int>(Color::Red);' and C++23 adds std::to_underlying.

questions.question: What is the default underlying type of an enum?
questions.correctAnswer: int
questions.explanation: Note: 'By default, the underlying type is int for both enum styles.'

questions.question: Why would you use `enum class Perm : uint8_t` instead of the default?
options: Save memory — 1 byte instead of 4 in arrays/structs
questions.correctAnswer: Save memory — 1 byte instead of 4 in arrays/structs
questions.explanation: Note: 'Use uint8_t for small enums. Saves memory when stored in arrays or as struct members.'

questions.question: What C++20 feature imports all values of an enum class into local scope?
questions.correctAnswer: using enum
questions.explanation: Note: 'C++20: using enum Axis; imports all Axis values into this scope. Use sparingly.'

questions.question: What is the common pattern for iterating over sequential enum values?
options: Add a Count sentinel value as last member, loop 0 to Count-1
questions.correctAnswer: Add a Count sentinel value as last member, loop 0 to Count-1
questions.explanation: Note: 'Add Count as sentinel. For i=0 to Count-1: cast i to enum. Fails for non-sequential values.'

questions.question: What is a reference in C++?
options: A copy of a variable on the heap
An alias — another name for an existing variable, not a copy and not a pointer
A const pointer that can never be null
questions.correctAnswer: An alias — another name for an existing variable, not a copy and not a pointer
questions.explanation: Note: 'A reference is an alias — another name for an existing variable. It's not a copy and not a pointer.'

questions.question: What happens when you assign to a reference?
options: The reference changes to point to a new variable
The original variable the reference aliases is modified
A copy is made, original unchanged
questions.correctAnswer: The original variable the reference aliases is modified
questions.explanation: Note: 'ref = 100; // Changes original to 100. They're the SAME variable.'

questions.question: Key difference between references and pointers?
options: References must be initialized, cannot be null, cannot be reassigned to alias something else
questions.correctAnswer: References must be initialized, cannot be null, cannot be reassigned to alias something else
questions.explanation: Note: 'References must be bound at declaration, cannot be null, and cannot be reassigned.'

questions.question: What is pass-by-reference used for?
options: Avoiding copies of large objects — zero-copy parameter passing
questions.correctAnswer: Avoiding copies of large objects — zero-copy parameter passing
questions.explanation: Note: 'References enable zero-copy parameter passing.' The function works directly on the caller's data.

questions.question: How are references implemented at the hardware level?
options: As a pointer under the hood — the compiler stores an address
questions.correctAnswer: As a pointer under the hood — the compiler stores an address
questions.explanation: Note: 'References are typically implemented as pointers under the hood — the compiler stores an address.'

questions.question: Why can't you have a reference to a reference?
options: The standard forbids it — references collapse: T& & becomes T&
questions.correctAnswer: The standard forbids it — references collapse: T& & becomes T&
questions.explanation: Note: Reference collapsing rules mean T& & collapses to T&. You can't have a reference-to-reference.

questions.question: What does `const int&` guarantee?
options: The reference itself can't be changed to alias something else
You cannot modify the referenced value through this reference
Both the reference and value are const
The value is stored in ROM
questions.correctAnswer: You cannot modify the referenced value through this reference
questions.explanation: Note: 'const int& means the referenced value cannot be modified through this reference.'

questions.question: When should you use a reference vs a pointer for function parameters?
options: Reference for required parameters that must exist; pointer for optional parameters (can be nullptr)
References for output; pointers for input
questions.correctAnswer: Reference for required parameters that must exist; pointer for optional parameters (can be nullptr)
questions.explanation: Note: References express 'this must exist.' Pointers can be null for optional parameters.

questions.question: How many distinct meanings does `static` have in C++?
questions.explanation: Note: 'One Keyword, Four Meanings': member variable, member function, local variable, global variable/function.

questions.question: What does a static member variable do?
options: One variable shared across ALL instances of the class
questions.correctAnswer: One variable shared across ALL instances of the class
questions.explanation: Note: 'Static Member Variables — One Copy, Shared By All.'

questions.question: What is the Static Initialization Order Fiasco?
options: Undefined construction order of global objects across .cpp files — crash before main()
questions.correctAnswer: Undefined construction order of global objects across .cpp files — crash before main()
questions.explanation: Note: 'Global static objects across multiple .cpp files have unspecified construction order. One reads another unconstructed — crash.'

questions.question: How does Meyer's Singleton (function-local static) solve the init order fiasco?
options: Defers initialization to the first function call, eliminating cross-TU ordering dependencies
questions.correctAnswer: Defers initialization to the first function call, eliminating cross-TU ordering dependencies
questions.explanation: Note: 'Function-local static defers to first call, eliminating ordering dependency between translation units.'

questions.question: What does C++17 `inline static` allow?
options: Define a static member variable directly in the class definition — no out-of-class .cpp definition needed
questions.correctAnswer: Define a static member variable directly in the class definition — no out-of-class .cpp definition needed
questions.explanation: Note: 'inline static — Definition AND declaration in one place! No .cpp definition required.'

questions.question: What does `static` at file/namespace scope do?
options: Internal linkage — visible only within this translation unit (.cpp file)
questions.correctAnswer: Internal linkage — visible only within this translation unit (.cpp file)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Static Global Variables/Functions — Internal Linkage. Private to this translation unit.'

questions.question: Since which C++ standard is static local initialization thread-safe?
questions.explanation: Note: 'Thread safety (C++11+): Static local initialization is guaranteed thread-safe via compiler-generated guard variable.'

questions.question: What C++20 keyword enforces compile-time initialization for static variables?
questions.correctAnswer: constinit
questions.explanation: Note: 'C++20 constinit: compile error if initialization requires runtime code — preventing the fiasco for compile-time-constructible values.'

questions.question: What types can structured bindings work with?
options: Pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members
Any type with a default constructor
questions.correctAnswer: Pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members
questions.explanation: Note: 'Structured bindings work with: pairs, tuples, arrays, and structs with all public members.'

questions.question: What three specializations enable custom types for structured bindings?
questions.correctAnswer: std::tuple_size, std::tuple_element, and get<I>()
questions.explanation: Note: specialize std::tuple_size (count), std::tuple_element<I,T> (type per index), provide get<I>(T) via ADL.

questions.question: What does the compiler generate for `auto [x, y] = GetPair();`?
options: A hidden copy with x and y as references into it
x and y moved from the pair
questions.correctAnswer: A hidden copy with x and y as references into it
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto __hidden = GetPair(); then references: x = get<0>(__hidden), y = get<1>(__hidden).'

questions.question: How does `const auto& [x, y]` differ from `auto [x, y]`?
options: x and y are const references — no copy is made
questions.correctAnswer: x and y are const references — no copy is made
questions.explanation: Note: 'const auto& [cx, cy, cz] = p; // const references — no copy.' Auto applies to hidden object.

questions.question: What C++20 feature uses structured bindings in a switch?
options: Switch with init-statement: switch (auto [code, msg] = GetResult(); code)
questions.correctAnswer: Switch with init-statement: switch (auto [code, msg] = GetResult(); code)
questions.explanation: Note: 'C++20: switch (auto [code, msg] = GetResult(); code) { case 0: ... case 404: ... }'

questions.question: In what order do structured bindings map to struct members?
questions.correctAnswer: Declaration order
questions.explanation: Note: 'Bindings map to member names in declaration order — NOT alphabetical.' First declared member = first binding.

questions.question: What is the aggregate protocol for structured bindings?
options: A special interface you must implement
If a type has only public members and no user-declared constructors, bindings work automatically
questions.correctAnswer: If a type has only public members and no user-declared constructors, bindings work automatically
questions.explanation: Note: 'Aggregate protocol: if all members are public and no user constructors, structured bindings work automatically.'

questions.question: What does `auto` strip from the deduced type?
questions.correctAnswer: References and top-level const
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto strips references and const (top-level). const int& → auto deduces int.'

questions.question: How do you make `auto` preserve references?
questions.correctAnswer: auto&
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto& r = GetValue(); // r is const int& — reference preserved.'

questions.question: What type does `decltype(x)` deduce for `int x = 5;`?
questions.correctAnswer: int
questions.explanation: Note: 'decltype deduces x's declared type — int.' Unlike auto, decltype preserves references and const.

questions.question: What is `decltype(auto)` used for?
options: Perfect return type forwarding — preserves references and const
questions.correctAnswer: Perfect return type forwarding — preserves references and const
questions.explanation: Note: 'decltype(auto) preserves everything. Use for perfect return type forwarding.'

questions.question: Why should you NOT use `for (auto x : vec)` to iterate?
options: It copies every element — use for (auto& x : vec)
questions.correctAnswer: It copies every element — use for (auto& x : vec)
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto never deduces to a reference. for (auto x : vec) copies every element.'

questions.question: What is the difference between `auto` and `decltype` regarding references?
options: auto strips references; decltype preserves them exactly as declared
decltype strips references; auto preserves them
auto is for variables; decltype is for functions
questions.correctAnswer: auto strips references; decltype preserves them exactly as declared
questions.explanation: Note: 'Unlike auto, decltype preserves references and const exactly as declared.'

questions.question: When is it NOT recommended to use auto?
options: When the return type isn't obvious from the initializer — the reader can't deduce what type it is
questions.correctAnswer: When the return type isn't obvious from the initializer — the reader can't deduce what type it is
questions.explanation: Note: 'auto result = initialize(); — reader has to deduce what initialize() returns. Make the type explicit.'

questions.question: What two mechanisms enable type conversion in C++?
questions.correctAnswer: Conversion operators (in the source type) and converting constructors (in the destination type)
questions.explanation: Note: 'Either gate: conversion operator inside source type, OR converting constructor inside destination type.'

questions.question: What does `explicit` do to a constructor or conversion operator?
options: Disables implicit use — still available via direct-init Dest d(src) or static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.correctAnswer: Disables implicit use — still available via direct-init Dest d(src) or static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.explanation: Note: 'explicit disables silent conversion. Direct-init and static_cast still work.'

questions.question: Why does `explicit operator bool` work in `if (ptr)`?
options: Contextual conversion — the standard grants a special exception for boolean contexts
questions.correctAnswer: Contextual conversion — the standard grants a special exception for boolean contexts
questions.explanation: Note: 'Contextual conversion: explicit operator bool CAN be used implicitly — but ONLY in boolean contexts (if, while, &&, ||, !, ?:).'

questions.question: How many user-defined conversions will the compiler chain implicitly?
questions.correctAnswer: One
questions.explanation: Note: 'At most ONE user-defined conversion in an implicit sequence. A→B→C requires two — rejected.'

questions.question: What does C++20 `explicit(bool)` allow?
options: Conditional explicitness based on a compile-time predicate like is_convertible_v
questions.correctAnswer: Conditional explicitness based on a compile-time predicate like is_convertible_v
questions.explanation: Note: 'explicit(!is_convertible_v<T,int>) — safe for float, guarded for string.'

questions.question: What happens if both a conversion operator and a converting constructor are non-explicit?
options: Ambiguity error — the compiler refuses to choose
questions.correctAnswer: Ambiguity error — the compiler refuses to choose
questions.explanation: Note: 'Both non-explicit → ambiguity error. Fix: make one explicit, or refactor to have only one path.'

questions.question: What contexts does `explicit` NOT block?
options: Direct-initialization Dest d(src) and explicit casts static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.correctAnswer: Direct-initialization Dest d(src) and explicit casts static_cast<Dest>(src)
questions.explanation: Note table shows: direct-initialization ✅ Allowed, explicit cast ✅ Allowed. Argument/return/copy-init are blocked.

questions.question: Why does UE prefer explicit on single-argument constructors?
options: Prevents accidental temporaries, makes allocations visible, defends against refactoring bugs
questions.correctAnswer: Prevents accidental temporaries, makes allocations visible, defends against refactoring bugs
questions.explanation: Note gives three reasons: prevents accidental temporaries, makes allocations visible, defends against refactoring bugs.
